original_abstract: Evaluating the socio-economic costs of road accidents is a critical component of traffic safety management, directly informed by European Union directives and national strategic planning. However, the reliability of these valuations varies significantly between member states due to differences in data accessibility and methodological rigor. This paper examines the fundamental methodological assumptions used to estimate unit costs of road occurrences, categorizing them into direct economic costs, indirect economic losses, and the value of safety per se. Central to the discussion is the complex and often controversial valuation of human life and the non-material impacts of accidents—such as physical pain, psychological suffering, and social exclusion. The author provides a comparative overview of established estimation techniques, including the human capital method, hedonistic pricing, and willingness-to-pay (WTP) models. Furthermore, the paper highlights the increasing relevance of court-awarded compensations and the "loss of joy of life" as essential factors in achieving more accurate Polish and international cost assessments. By bridging economic theory with social policy, the study emphasizes that a precise valuation of human life is not only an econometric necessity for infrastructure investment analysis but also a vital tool for increasing public awareness of road safety.
humanized_abstract: Determining the socio-economic consequences of road accidents is one of the basic tasks in the field of road safety and is strictly defined by EU directives and national road safety strategies. Unfortunately, the accuracy of the assessment is very different in individual EU countries, due to, among other things, different levels of data availability and different methods used for their determination. This article attempts to assess the assumptions that underpin the methods of determining the unit value of road accidents and provides an overview of the basic concepts of costs: direct economic costs, indirect economic costs and the value of safety. Special attention is paid to the valuation of human life and non-material injuries, such as injury, illness or stigma resulting from an accident. An overview of the most commonly used methods for valuation, including: human capital method, hedonistic pricing and willingness-to-pay (WTP) methods, is also presented. The court awards for damage to life and health, as well as the loss of joy of life are also shown as important factors in determining the costs of road accidents in Poland and internationally. In conclusion, the article bridges the field of economic science and social policy, showing that an exact valuation of human life is an econometric necessity in relation to the evaluation of investments in infrastructure objects, and is also a very important tool for improving the knowledge of road accidents among the public.
